answers.q3: Order: recover → body-limit → request-logger → auth. The recover middleware wraps all panics into the JSON error envelope format. Body limit runs before auth to prevent large payload abuse.
answers.q4: Yes — generate UUID per request, log it, and return it in X-Request-ID response header. Propagate incoming X-Request-ID if present.
answers.q5: Treat as fatal: log the error and exit immediately (os.Exit(1)). The admin token is the only recovery mechanism on a fresh deployment; losing it silently is unacceptable.
answers.q6: Parse and store the value but do not validate it in spec 1. Validation happens in spec 2 when it is actually used for OAuth.
answers.q7: Testing strategy is specified via the spec CLI's test_spec.json artifact. The spec generate step will produce test contracts.

answers.q1:
Here is the annotated config.toml:

[server]
port = 8080
bind = "0.0.0.0"
external_url = ""  # optional, used for OAuth redirect URI in production

[database]
path = "./data/af-hub.db"

[log]
level = "info"  # trace|debug|info|warn|error|fatal|panic

[[oauth.providers]]
name = "github"
client_id = "your-github-client-id"
client_secret = "your-github-client-secret"
authorize_url = ""  # optional, defaults to GitHub well-known URL
token_url = ""      # optional
userinfo_url = ""   # optional
answers.q2: The proposed order is correct: (1) Parse CLI flags, (2) Load config.toml, (3) Initialize structured logging, (4) Open/initialize SQLite + apply pragmas + create tables, (5) Run admin bootstrap or token validation, (6) Register HTTP routes and middleware, (7) Start HTTP listener, (8) Begin listening for SIGTERM/SIGINT.
answers.q3: Create the directory automatically (including parents, like mkdir -p).
answers.q4: Treat as first boot — generate and write new token, ignore missing env var. Zero rows in admin_tokens is the canonical first-boot signal.
answers.q5: All four cases return HTTP 401 with the same generic message: 'missing or invalid authentication credentials'. No information leakage about what went wrong.
answers.q6: Keep external artifact as source of truth. The spec generate step produces test_spec.json with full test contracts. No inline test table needed.

answers.q1: Use the current working directory (i.e., ./admin_token). The current directory is where config.toml is expected by default.
answers.q2: Application logic only (delete before insert during rotation). A single-row table constraint is overkill for a table managed by a single code path.
answers.q3: Return HTTP 401 immediately if the parsed parts don't match the expected count or character length/format. No unnecessary DB queries for structurally invalid tokens.
answers.q4: Remain running indefinitely — let the orchestrator decide what to do (e.g., restart via liveness probe). This is the standard Kubernetes pattern.
answers.q5: Always stdout. This is the standard 12-factor app pattern for container and systemd environments.
answers.q6: No — --reset-admin-token is an operator escape hatch and bypasses env var validation. This is intentional: the operator has physical access to the server and uses this flag when they've lost the current token.
answers.q7: GET and HEAD are supported; all others return HTTP 405.
answers.q8: Leave testing entirely to the generated artifact — no fallback needed. The spec generate step always runs as part of the spec workflow.

answers.q1: Log a fatal error and exit immediately. A present-but-invalid config is a deployment mistake and must not be silently ignored.
answers.q2: Permissive mode: log a warning for each unknown field and continue with defaults. This catches typos without blocking startup during rapid iteration.
answers.q3: Use a short fixed timeout (2 seconds). Fail with 503 if the query doesn't return within that window.
answers.q4: Register auth middleware only on a named route group (e.g., api := e.Group('/api/v1'), protected := api.Group('', authMiddleware)) so spec 2 can add auth routes to the unprotected group without modifying this spec's code.
answers.q5: Log a single info-level entry at listener start with fields: bind, port, db_path, log_level.
answers.q6: Echo's default 405 behavior is acceptable — no custom handling required. Health probes are infrastructure, not API.
answers.q7: The file persists — operator's responsibility. Log a security warning at warn level on every boot if the file still exists.
answers.q8: Require logrus v1.4.0+ for native trace support. Document in go.mod.
answers.q9: Keep the reference to test_spec.json as-is. The spec CLI is a required part of the development workflow. It will be run as the next step after this refinement completes.

answers.q1: Log only the first failure at error level; subsequent consecutive failures at debug level until recovery. Prevents log flooding.
answers.q2: HTTP 503 with {"error": {"code": 503, "message": "service temporarily unavailable"}}. DB contention is a transient condition.
answers.q3: Any PRAGMA failure is fatal: log the specific PRAGMA that failed with the reason, then exit (os.Exit(1)). WAL mode failure in particular means the DB is unusable.
answers.q4: Warn and default to info. Log a warning with the invalid value, then use info. Non-fatal because the server can still function with a reasonable log level.
answers.q5: Single connection for writes, unbounded pool for reads (standard Go sql.DB behavior with WAL). This is the default behavior of Go's database/sql with SQLite in WAL mode.
answers.q6: Yes — file write failure during rotation is fatal, identical to first-boot behavior.
answers.q7: No — the spec generate CLI is the single source of truth. The CLI is a required part of the workflow.

answers.q1: spec CLI is a hard prerequisite. It will be run immediately after this step. The tool is already installed.
answers.q2: Accept any non-empty printable ASCII string up to 128 characters; generate a new UUID if empty or invalid.
answers.q3: Register a global custom error handler on the Echo instance that enforces the envelope for all non-health-probe routes.

answers.q1: SHA-256 of the raw secret component only (32 alphanumeric chars). The key_id/token_id is used for lookup; the secret is hashed for verification.
answers.q2: Log an info-level notice that the env var is set but will be ignored on first boot.
answers.q3: Use UTC; token is expired if expires_at < now (exclusive boundary — token is valid until the expiry instant has passed).
answers.q4: Remain silent — no log entry when the file is absent.
answers.q5: Use Echo's built-in server shutdown (http.Server.Shutdown) which tracks connections internally; log a fixed message without an exact count.
answers.q7: Retain as-is — it is a process gate, not an implementation requirement.
